title: Today's Tech Brief: Google's AI Push and Data Center Realities
summary: Google's AI enshittification, data centers' resource needs, and the implications for the tech industry.
imageAlt: Image from source article for Google has seriously leaned into AI enshittification lately
imageCaption: Google has seriously leaned into AI enshittification lately
imageSourceTitle: Google has seriously leaned into AI enshittification lately

body.text: I'm Leo Stone, and welcome to Today's Tech Brief. I've been digging into some interesting developments in the tech world, and I'm excited to share them with you. I've got a few stories that caught my attention recently, and I think they're worth chatting about.

So, Google has been making a big push into AI, which some are calling 'enshittification.' This means they're integrating AI into their search results, which could change how we interact with the web. I'm not sure about you, but I sometimes feel like I'm already getting a little too much AI-generated content in my search results. The question is, will this change make Google search better or worse?

According to a report from The Register, Google's aggressive AI integration might backfire. The idea is that if users don't like what they see, they might start using other search engines. It's a bit like if you walked into a restaurant and the menu was completely changed – you might not be too happy about it. I'm curious to see how this plays out.

New reports are also highlighting the resource needs of data centers, which are the backbone of our digital world. Axios has some sobering numbers on just how much energy and water these centers require. It's a bit like realizing that your favorite streaming service uses as much energy as a small town – it's a lot to take in. This makes me think about the environmental impact of our tech habits.

My quick read: today's tech stories are less about one giant thunderclap and more about a lively weather pattern. A little platform drama here, a little artificial intelligence irony there, and suddenly the industry has enough material for a very caffeinated morning show.
